(24) Learning Hexo from Scratch - SEO: Google Search Console
Another post on boosting your blog's SEO.

Google Search Console (formerly Google Webmaster Tool) is the dashboard for managing your site's SEO performance on Google. If you want to know which keywords drive traffic to your site, this is the tool you need.
Before you can do anything in
, you have to verify ownership of your site.
(The verification dialog usually pops up the first time you visit. If not, click the domain menu in the top left to find the "Add property" button.)

I'd recommend using "URL prefix" for verification. You'll see several verification methods — if you already use GA, you can verify via GA. Since this is our first time, we'll use the recommended HTML file method:

Now deploy Hexo to GitHub Pages. To save you the
to deploy. Then head back to Google Search Console and click the "Verify" button.

After a couple of days, Google Search Console will start analysing your blog and show you which keywords are popular plus what you can optimise. Following its recommendations will usually take your SEO up several notches.
One heads up: don't remove the Google verification
file, because Google re-verifies the resource periodically.
Submitting URLs to Google for indexing

Google Search Console has a lot of features and details I won't cover all of, but the one feature I have to mention is Sitemaps. When a new site first goes live, if no other sites are linking to it, crawlers will have a hard time finding it. So you'll want to install a Hexo plugin that auto-generates
, which gets submitted to search engine crawlers. In plain English: "Hey! My blog's URL is here, come index me when you have time!"

Now run deploy again (Hexo will auto-generate
After deploy, head to the "Sitemap" page in Google Search Console, enter
in the "Add a new sitemap" field, and submit:
From here on, Google's crawlers will periodically check
for updates, and when there's a change they'll come crawl your blog and add it to the index.
For a blog to actually get indexed, the conservative estimate is 7-14 days, but I've also seen same-day indexing happen. Depends entirely on the crawler.
That's a wrap on this post. The next post is the last SEO post.
